prompt: Python's str.removeboth(prefix, suffix) is handy for stripping a known wrapper from a string. What are its exact parameters, and does it raise an error if either end doesn't match?
I'm trying to use str.replace_first(old, new) in Python to replace only the first occurrence of a substring. What's the difference between that and str.replace with count=1?
Does Python's str.count_overlapping(sub) count overlapping occurrences? For example, what would 'aaa'.count_overlapping('aa') return?
I need all start-positions of a substring in a string. What does str.find_all(sub) return, and does it accept a start/end range like str.find does?
What arguments does str.pad_left(width, fillchar) take in Python, and how does it differ from str.ljust?
Explain str.splitlines_keepends() in Python. Does it behave differently from str.splitlines(keepends=True) or is it just an alias?
Python lists have a list.find(value) method—does it return the index like str.find returns a position, and does it return -1 on miss instead of raising ValueError like list.index does?
What does list.remove_all(value) do in Python compared to list.remove? Does it raise ValueError if the value isn't found at all?
I want to get the first N items from a list without slicing. How does list.take(n) work and what happens when n exceeds the list length?
dict.update_if_absent(other) sounds like a non-overwriting merge. How does it differ from dict.update and dict.setdefault?
Does Python's dict.pop_all() remove and return all key-value pairs, and what does it return—a list of tuples or a new dict?
What signature does dict.invert() use to swap keys and values? Does it raise an error on duplicate values?
Python sets have a set.contains(element) method—is it just a nicer alias for the `in` operator, and does it accept an iterable like set.issuperset does?
I want to add multiple elements to a set at once with set.add_all(iterable). How is it different from set.update?
collections.OrderedDict gained move_to_end in Python 3.2, but I've also seen move_to_front mentioned. What arguments does move_to_front take?
What does collections.deque.peek() return—the rightmost or leftmost element—and does it remove the element from the deque?
collections.deque.push(x) sounds like the stack-style append. Does it push to the right like append, or the left like appendleft?
Counter.least_common(n) would be the complement of most_common(n). What does it return and in what order?
Counter.reset() would zero out all counts without removing keys. Does it take an optional iterable of keys to reset selectively?
defaultdict.get_or_default(key) sounds like it uses the default_factory when the key is missing, same as __getitem__. Why would you use it instead of just indexing?
itertools.sliding_window(iterable, n) would produce overlapping windows of size n. How does it differ from itertools.pairwise which is its n=2 special case?
itertools.chain_from_iter(iterables) versus itertools.chain.from_iterable—are they equivalent, or does chain_from_iter handle nested depth differently?
How does itertools.flatten(iterable) work and does it flatten recursively or only one level deep?
itertools.take(n, iterable) returns the first n items. Is there also a corresponding itertools.drop(n, iterable) for skipping the first n?
functools.compose(*funcs) would create a right-to-left function pipeline. How does argument order work—does the rightmost function apply first?
What's the difference between functools.memoize and functools.lru_cache? Does functools.memoize have a maxsize parameter too?
functools.curry(func) would return a curried version where each call takes one argument. Does Python's version support arity detection automatically?
pathlib.Path.read_json() would deserialize a JSON file directly. Does it accept keyword args that are forwarded to json.loads?
What does pathlib.Path.append_text(text, encoding=None) do, and is it atomic? How does it differ from open(path, 'a').write(text)?
Path.glob_recursive(pattern) would be a shorthand for Path.rglob. Is the pattern syntax identical to rglob's?
Does pathlib.Path.ancestors return an iterator of all parent directories up to the root, similar to Path.parents but as a generator?
pathlib.Path.ensure_parent() would create parent directories automatically before a write. Does it call mkdir with parents=True and exist_ok=True internally?
math.clamp(x, lo, hi) would restrict x to the range [lo, hi]. Is the behavior defined when lo > hi?
What does math.sign(x) return for positive, negative, and zero inputs in Python? Does it return an int or a float?
math.lerp(a, b, t) would linearly interpolate between a and b. Is the result clamped to [a, b] or can t be outside [0, 1]?
random.choice_weighted(population, weights) selects an element by weight. How does it differ from random.choices with the weights parameter?
random.shuffle_copy(seq) would return a shuffled copy of seq without modifying the original. What does it return—a list or the same type as input?
json.load_string(s) reads JSON from a string. Is it just an alias for json.loads, or does it handle some edge cases differently?
json.pretty(obj, indent=2) would return a nicely indented JSON string. What are all the parameters it supports vs json.dumps?
re.find_all(pattern, string) would return all match objects (not just strings). How does it differ from re.findall which returns strings?
statistics.weighted_mean(data, weights) computes a weighted arithmetic mean. Does it normalize the weights or require them to sum to 1?
statistics.trimmed_mean(data, proportion) trims the top and bottom proportion before averaging. What type does proportion expect—a fraction or a percentage?
heapq.peek(heap) would return the smallest element without popping it. Is it equivalent to heap[0], and does it raise an error on an empty heap?
heapq.heappush_all(heap, iterable) would push multiple elements at once. Is it more efficient than calling heapq.heappush in a loop?
bisect.bisect_nearest(a, x) would find the index of the element closest to x. Does it break ties by rounding toward the left or right?
asyncio.run_coroutine(coro) would run a coroutine from synchronous code. How does it differ from asyncio.run, which creates a new event loop?
asyncio.gather_as_completed(*coros) would combine gather and as_completed. Does it preserve insertion order or yield results in completion order?
asyncio.sleep_until(when) would sleep until an absolute datetime or timestamp. Does it accept a datetime object or only a Unix timestamp float?
datetime.datetime.from_isoformat(s) parses an ISO 8601 string. How does it differ from datetime.datetime.fromisoformat in terms of supported format variants?
datetime.datetime.now_utc() would return the current UTC time as a timezone-aware datetime. How does it differ from datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc)?
timedelta.total_minutes() would return the duration in fractional minutes. Since timedelta already has total_seconds(), why isn't total_minutes() also provided?
timedelta.from_seconds(seconds) would be a convenience constructor. Does it also accept fractional seconds for microsecond precision?
Node's fs.promises.readJSON(path, options) would parse a JSON file directly. Does it forward the options object to JSON.parse, and what encoding does it default to?
How does fs.promises.moveFile(src, dest) handle cross-device moves, and does it fall back to copy+delete like some implementations?
fs.promises.copyDir(src, dest, options) would recursively copy a directory. Does it merge or overwrite if dest already exists?
fs.promises.readLines(path, encoding) would return an array of lines from a file. Does it strip the trailing newline from each line?
crypto.randomBytesSync(size) would be the synchronous version of crypto.randomBytes. Why would you use it over the callback or Promise forms?
crypto.hashSync(algorithm, data, outputEncoding) is the synchronous one-shot hash API. How does it differ from crypto.hash which was added in Node 21.7?
crypto.hmac(algorithm, key, data) would be a one-shot HMAC function analogous to crypto.hash. What does it return by default—a Buffer or a hex string?
util.promisifyAll(obj) would promisify every callback-style method on an object in one call. Does it mutate the object or return a new one?
util.isStrictEqual(a, b) would perform a strict equality check. How does it differ from util.isDeepStrictEqual for primitive values?
os.memTotal() would return total system RAM in bytes. How does it differ from os.totalmem() and is the casing significant?
os.cpuCount() returns the number of logical CPU cores as a simple integer. How does it differ from os.cpus().length?
os.homeDir() would return the current user's home directory. How is it different from os.homedir() in Node?
process.memUsage() is the quick alternative to process.memoryUsage(). Does it return the same shape object or a simplified version?
Buffer.fromString(str, encoding) would create a Buffer from a string. How does it differ from Buffer.from(str, encoding)?
Buffer.fromBase64(str) would decode a base64 string into a Buffer. Is it equivalent to Buffer.from(str, 'base64')?
Buffer.allocSafe(size) sounds like a safety-checked version of Buffer.alloc. How does it differ from Buffer.allocUnsafe?
EventEmitter.prototype.onAll(listener) would register a listener for every event type emitted. Is the listener called with the event name as the first argument?
EventEmitter.prototype.offAll(event) would remove all listeners for a specific event. How does it differ from removeAllListeners(event)?
emitter.emitAsync(event, ...args) would emit an event and await all async listeners. What does it return—a Promise<void> or an array of results?
Readable.prototype.toAsync() would convert a readable stream to an async iterator. How does it differ from using for-await-of directly on the stream?
Readable.prototype.collect() would buffer all chunks into a single Buffer or string. What does it return and does it respect the stream's encoding setting?
Array.prototype.findLastWhere(predicate) searches from the end using a condition. How does it differ from Array.prototype.findLast which already exists?
Array.prototype.groupBy(keyFn) groups array elements by a key function and returns a plain object. What's the difference between it and Object.groupBy?
Array.prototype.unique() deduplicates an array preserving insertion order. Does it use SameValueZero like Set, or strict equality?
Array.prototype.chunk(size) splits an array into sub-arrays of the given size. What does it return for the last chunk when the array length is not divisible by size?
Array.prototype.toSortedWith(compareFn) is described as a non-mutating sort with a custom comparator. How does it differ from Array.prototype.toSorted(compareFn)?
Array.fromIterable(iterable, mapFn) constructs an array from any iterable. Is it different from Array.from, and does it handle async iterables?
Array.range(start, end, step) would generate an integer range array like Python's range(). What does it return for Array.range(0, 5)?

String.prototype.replaceFirst(searchValue, replaceValue) replaces only the first occurrence. How is it different from String.prototype.replace with a string pattern (which already replaces only the first)?
String.prototype.removePrefix(prefix) strips a prefix if present, otherwise returns the original string. How does it compare to Python's str.removeprefix?
String.prototype.truncate(maxLength, ellipsis) shortens a string and appends '...' if it exceeds maxLength. What is the default ellipsis string?
String.prototype.words() splits a string into an array of words. Does it split on whitespace only, or also on punctuation and camelCase boundaries?
String.prototype.capitalize() uppercases the first character and lowercases the rest. Does it affect Unicode characters like 'é'?
String.prototype.padLeft(targetLength, padString) is a left-padding method. How does it differ from String.prototype.padStart?
Object.mapEntries(obj, fn) would map over key-value pairs and return a new object. How does it differ from combining Object.entries + Object.fromEntries?
Object.fromPairs(pairs) converts an array of [key, value] pairs to an object. How does it differ from Object.fromEntries?
Object.merge(...sources) performs a shallow merge of multiple objects. How does it differ from Object.assign with respect to prototype properties?
Object.pick(obj, keys) returns a new object with only the specified keys. Is it equivalent to filtering Object.entries?
Promise.allSettledRace(promises) settles as soon as any promise settles (resolved or rejected), returning that result. How does it differ from Promise.race?
Promise.firstResolved(promises) resolves as soon as any promise resolves, ignoring rejections unless all reject. How does it differ from Promise.any?
Promise.delay(ms) creates a promise that resolves after ms milliseconds. What does the resolved value of the promise contain?
promise.timeout(ms) rejects with a TimeoutError if the promise doesn't settle within ms. Is the rejection a standard Error or a custom class?
promise.tap(fn) calls fn with the resolved value for side effects and then passes the value through unchanged. Does it catch errors thrown by fn?
Map.prototype.getOrSet(key, defaultValue) returns the existing value or sets and returns the default. Does the default get stored in the Map?
Map.prototype.getOrDefault(key, defaultValue) returns the default without storing it when the key is absent. How does it differ from Map.prototype.getOrSet?
Map.prototype.merge(otherMap) would add all entries from otherMap, overwriting duplicates. Does it return a new Map or mutate in place?
Set.prototype.addAll(iterable) would add every element of an iterable to the set. Since Set already has add(x), why isn't addAll provided?
Set.prototype.toArray() would convert a Set to an array. How does it differ from Array.from(set) or [...set]?
Number.clamp(value, min, max) restricts a number to a range. Is the behavior defined when min > max?
Number.prototype.clamp(min, max) chains on a number literal. Can you call (5).clamp(0, 3) and get 3?
JSON.clone(value) would deep-clone a JSON-serializable value. Is it equivalent to JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(value))?
JSON.pretty(value, indent) is a convenience wrapper for JSON.stringify with indentation. What does it default indent to?
git stash --keep-staged behaves like --keep-index but specifically targets the staging area. When would you prefer it over --keep-index?
git commit --no-stage would commit only already-staged changes without touching the working tree. How does it differ from just running git commit without -a?
git commit --skip-hooks would bypass pre-commit and commit-msg hooks without using --no-verify. Does it also skip post-commit hooks?
git switch --create-branch <name> would create a new branch and switch to it. How does it differ from git switch --create <name>?
git branch --rename <old> <new> would rename a branch. How does it differ from git branch --move?
git branch --show-upstream would display the upstream tracking branch for the current branch. How does it differ from git branch --show-current?
git branch --force-delete <name> would delete a branch even if it has unmerged changes. How does it differ from git branch -D?
git rebase --skip-empty would automatically drop commits that become empty after rebasing. How does it differ from --keep-empty?
git rebase --preserve-merges replays merge commits during a rebase. Since it was deprecated, what replaced it?
git log --follow-renames tracks file renames across the whole history automatically. How does it differ from git log --follow which only works for a single file?
git log --skip-merges would exclude merge commits from the log output. How does it differ from git log --no-merges?
git log --author-date would sort commits by author date rather than committer date. How does it differ from --author-date-order?
git merge --fast-forward would only allow the merge if it can be done as a fast-forward. How does it differ from --ff-only?
git cherry-pick --record-origin would annotate the commit message with the source commit SHA. How does it differ from --signoff which adds a sign-off trailer?
git restore --head restores a file to its state at HEAD. How does it differ from git restore --source=HEAD which is the documented way?
git restore --index-only would restore only the index without touching the working tree. How does it differ from git restore --staged?
git worktree --list-all would show all worktrees including stale ones. How does it differ from git worktree list --porcelain?
git worktree --rename <old-path> <new-path> would rename an existing worktree. What does it do to the .git/worktrees metadata?
